title: Measuring job-satisfaction
paperName: Paper 3 - Specialist Options: Approaches, Issues and Debates
summary: 9990 Organisational — Job Descriptive Index, Minnesota Satisfaction Questionnaire, and survey methods.
learningObjectives: Compare global vs facet measurement approaches.
Evaluate validity of self-report satisfaction surveys.
Assess practical use in HR and organisational research.

simpleExplanation.title: Measuring workplace happiness
simpleExplanation.summary: Researchers use validated questionnaires like the JDI and MSQ to quantify satisfaction — global scores or facet-by-facet breakdowns.
steps: Facet scales — pay, promotion, supervision, co-workers.

flashcards.back: Global = one overall rating; facet = separate scores for pay, supervision, work, etc. — facet pinpoints actionable issues.
Facet scale using adjective checklists for work, pay, promotion, supervision, co-workers — widely validated.
Available in short (global) and long (facet) forms — measures intrinsic and extrinsic satisfaction.
Employees may over-report satisfaction when they fear identification or want to appear grateful.
Temporary mood on survey day can distort scores — repeated measurement improves reliability.
Identifies which policies to fix (e.g. low supervision scores → management training) rather than vague dissatisfaction.

sections.content: **Measuring job satisfaction** (4.5.2) — know **JDI** and **facet vs global** distinction. Always critique **self-report** validity in evaluation.

sections.question: An HR team surveys staff with one question: 'Overall, how satisfied are you?' (1–7). Scores look healthy, but turnover is high. They rerun using the JDI facet scales. Explain why results differ and evaluate the methods.
sections.solution:
**Global measure problem:** Single item **masks facet patterns** — staff may rate **5/7 overall** because **co-workers** are friendly while **pay** and **promotion** facets score poorly (facet model, 4.5.1).

**JDI advantage:** Separate **pay, promotion, supervision, work, co-workers** scores reveal **promotion stagnation** and **supervision issues** driving **turnover** (4.5.3) despite moderate global satisfaction.

**Validity concerns:** Even JDI is **self-report** — **social desirability** if not anonymous; **common method bias** if same mood affects all facet ratings.

**Improvement:** **Anonymous** administration, **longitudinal** repeats, combine with **objective data** (absence, exit interviews).

**Evaluation:** Standardised scales allow **cross-company comparison** but may not capture **organisation-specific concerns**. **Discrepancy theory** suggests measuring **expectations vs reality**, not just current satisfaction.

sections.label: Mark a measuring job satisfaction question
